flashcards.front: Explain the 'double-edged sword' concept of the immune system.
flashcards.back: Immunity is a defense mechanism against infections, but it can also become injurious to the body through hypersensitivity, autoimmunity, or amyloidosis [1, 2].

flashcards.front: Differentiate between Innate and Adaptive immunity components.
flashcards.back: Innate (Natural): Non-specific, first line of defense; includes complement (humoral) and neutrophils/NK cells (cellular). Adaptive (Specific): Characterized by antigenic specificity; includes antibodies (humoral) and T cells (cellular) [3].

flashcards.front: Which chromosome contains the HLA (Major Histocompatibility Complex) system?
flashcards.back: Chromosome 6, specifically on the short arm (p) [4, 5].

flashcards.front: Match the MHC Classes with their specific T-cell interactions.
flashcards.back: MHC Class I (HLA-A, B, C) interacts with CD8+ (T-suppressor/cytotoxic) cells. MHC Class II (HLA-DR, DQ, DP) interacts with CD4+ (T-helper) cells [6, 7].

flashcards.front: What is 'MHC Restriction'?
flashcards.back: The requirement for an antigen to be presented to T cells in association with the body's own MHC molecules to be recognized as 'non-self' [8, 9].

flashcards.front: Define the four types of grafts based on genetic relationship.
flashcards.back: 1. Autografts (same individual). 2. Isografts (same genotype/twins). 3. Allografts (same species, different genotype). 4. Xenografts (different species) [10].

flashcards.front: Contrast the timing and mechanism of Hyperacute vs. Acute graft rejection.
flashcards.back: Hyperacute: Occurs within minutes/hours; mediated by preformed humoral antibodies. Acute: Occurs within days/months; mediated by either T-cell cellular response or humoral vasculitis [11-13].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Graft-versus-host (GVH) reaction'?
flashcards.back: Occurs when immunocompetent cells (usually bone marrow) are transplanted into an immunodeficient recipient, causing the graft to attack the host [14, 15].

flashcards.front: What is the diagnostic threshold for AIDS according to the CDC classification?
flashcards.back: A CD4+ T cell count of <200/μl, regardless of the presence of clinical symptoms [16, 17].

flashcards.front: Identify the primary HIV surface proteins involved in host cell entry.
flashcards.back: gp120 (selective tropism for the CD4 receptor) and gp41 (mediates internalisation/fusion) [18-20].

flashcards.front: Critical Thinking: Why is a negative ELISA in the first 2-4 weeks of HIV exposure potentially misleading?
flashcards.back: This is the 'window period.' The patient is highly infectious but hasn't yet produced detectable antibodies (seroconversion). Direct detection of p24 antigen or HIV RNA is required for early diagnosis [21, 22].

flashcards.front: Differentiate Type I and Type IV Hypersensitivity by timing and mediators.
flashcards.back: Type I (Anaphylactic): Immediate (15-30 mins); mediated by IgE binding to mast cells. Type IV (Delayed): Slow onset (24-48 hours); mediated by T cells/cytokines [23-25].

flashcards.front: What is the hallmark morphological finding of Type III Hypersensitivity?
flashcards.back: Fibrinoid necrosis in blood vessel walls due to the deposition of antigen-antibody (immune) complexes [26, 27].

flashcards.front: Name the most specific antibody tests for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).
flashcards.back: Anti-dsDNA (70% specific) and Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) antibodies (25% specific) [28, 29].

flashcards.front: What is the 'LE cell phenomenon'?
flashcards.back: A neutrophil that has phagocytosed a denatured nucleus (LE body) coated with antinuclear antibodies; a diagnostic hallmark of SLE [30, 31].

flashcards.front: Bias Recognition: Why should you not diagnose Amyloidosis based solely on H&E staining?
flashcards.back: Amyloid appears as pink, eosinophilic 'hyaline' material on H&E, but many other substances (protein deposits, scars) look identical. Specificity requires Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence under polarized light [32-34].

flashcards.front: Explain the difference between AL and AA Amyloidosis etiology.
flashcards.back: AL (Primary): Derived from immunoglobulin light chains; associated with B-cell/plasma cell disorders. AA (Secondary): Derived from SAA protein; associated with chronic inflammation or infections [35-38].

flashcards.front: Clinical Case: A patient with chronic tuberculosis develops proteinuria and enlarged kidneys. What is the most likely diagnosis?
flashcards.back: Secondary (AA) Amyloidosis of the kidneys, triggered by the long-standing inflammatory stimulus of tuberculosis [39, 40].

flashcards.front: Differentiate 'Sago Spleen' from 'Lardaceous Spleen' in Amyloidosis.
flashcards.back: Sago Spleen: Deposits involve the white pulp (follicles); grossly resembles sago grains. Lardaceous Spleen: Deposits involve the red pulp (sinusoids); grossly shows map-like waxy areas [41, 42].

flashcards.front: What biochemical protein is associated with Senile Cerebral Amyloidosis (Alzheimer's)?
flashcards.back: Amyloid β-peptide (Aβ), derived from amyloid beta precursor protein (AβPP) [43, 44].

flashcards.front: Problem-Solving: How can you distinguish AL from AA amyloid using the permanganate test?
flashcards.back: Congophilia (affinity for Congo red) persists in AL amyloid after potassium permanganate treatment but is lost in AA amyloid [45, 46].

flashcards.front: USMLE Pattern: Which HLA association is most strongly linked to Ankylosing Spondylitis?
What are the components of the 'Sicca Syndrome' in Sjögren's Syndrome?
flashcards.back: The combination of dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca) and dry mouth (xerostomia) [47].

flashcards.front: Which diagnostic procedure is currently favored for systemic amyloidosis diagnosis due to its high yield and low invasiveness?
flashcards.back: Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of abdominal subcutaneous fat [48, 49].
